vitals.field_size: 850+ starters (organizer did not publish a precise total)
vitals.entry_fee: Late entry EUR 70; on-site entry EUR 100 (non-refundable)

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 79. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 74.

course_description.character: An all-asphalt loop leaving Aquagranda in Livigno (already at 1,816 m), crossing the town and climbing the Forcola di Livigno into Switzerland to reach Passo Bernina. The route continues through the Upper Engadin to Zernez, crosses Pass dal Fuorn and the Umbrail Pass, then descends toward the Bormio/Valdidentro side of the Rhaetian Alps. The decisive return climbs Passo del Foscagno and Passo d'Eira before a steep finishing road alongside the Mottolino ski slope. Classification is the sum of designated climbing-sector times — between timed climbs, riders ride under normal road rules, not a continuous race clock.
course_description.signature_challenge: The Eira-Mottolino finale (1.9 km at roughly 9.3%) to the 2,385 m Mottolino gondola station — the same finish where Tadej Pogačar soloed the 2024 Giro d'Italia queen stage, now the granfondo's dedicated closing test.
suffering_zones: Umbrail Pass — 13.1 km at roughly 8.6% average, arriving after more than 100 km already ridden
Foscagno — 15.1 km from Isolaccia at roughly 6.3% average
Eira-Mottolino finale — 1.9 km at roughly 9.3% average to the 2,385 m finish, the steepest section concentrated right at the line

logistics.lodging_strategy: Abundant in Livigno, Europe's cycling capital with training camps; book early due to 2026 Olympics proximity.

history.origin_story: Ran in 2024 as 'Granfondo Alé La Merckx' for its first Livigno edition; by 2026 the official regulations and ENDU listing had dropped the La Merckx name in favor of 'Granfondo Livigno Alé.'
notable_moments: 2024: inaugural Livigno edition branded 'Alé La Merckx,' drawing roughly 800 riders on the climbs Pogačar had just soloed at the Giro

biased_opinion.verdict: Real Altitude, Small Field, Confusing Branding
biased_opinion.summary: Livigno sits at 1,816 m and is a legitimate high-altitude training hub, which gives this granfondo a genuine cycling identity rather than a manufactured one — the 166.8 km/4,300 m route links major Rhaetian Alps passes across Italy and Switzerland with sustained riding above 2,000 m, and the Eira-Mottolino finale climbs the exact slope where Tadej Pogačar soloed the 2024 Giro d'Italia queen stage. But it's a modestly sized field (850+ starters in 2026) for a heavily promoted international event, and the branding has been inconsistent — 2024's 'Granfondo Alé La Merckx' became 2026's 'Granfondo Livigno Alé,' with older pages and search results for the old name still circulating. Classification only counts designated climbing sectors, not the full 166.8 km, so this isn't a continuous timed race either.
strengths: Genuine high-altitude cycling identity — Livigno at 1,816 m, sustained riding above 2,000 m across Italian and Swiss Rhaetian Alps passes
The Eira-Mottolino finale directly retraces the 2024 Giro d'Italia queen-stage finish where Pogačar soloed to the win
Multiple named passes (Bernina, Umbrail, Foscagno, Eira) create a genuinely demanding, varied high-mountain course
Mediofondo option (106.5 km/2,050 m) for riders who want the altitude and scenery without the full distance
weaknesses: Remote high-altitude border resort location means more planning for transfers, accommodation, and weather contingencies than a valley-floor event
850+ starters is a relatively small field for how heavily the event is promoted internationally
Branding has shifted from 'Granfondo Alé La Merckx' (2024) to 'Granfondo Livigno Alé' (2026) with no clean transition — old pages and search results for the former name persist
Only selected climbing sectors are timed; it is not a conventional continuous-time road race
Entry fees are non-refundable (EUR 70 late / EUR 100 on-site), and foreign riders need a competitive cycling medical certificate
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you want genuine high-altitude Alpine passes and the actual Pogačar finishing climb, this delivers — just come in knowing the field is modest, the branding has changed recently, and only the climbs are timed.

final_verdict.one_liner: Ultimate high-alpine road suffering with notable scenery and climbs.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want genuine high-altitude Rhaetian Alps climbing and the real Pogačar finishing slope. The field is modest and only climbing sectors are timed, not the full route.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a similarly high-altitude Alpine granfondo with a larger, more established field, look at other Dolomites/Alps events with longer competitive track records.

videos.title: Alla scoperta dei percorsi della GF Alé La Merckx a Livigno
videos.description:
Il 29 giugno non prendere impegni: ti aspetta la rinnovata Granfondo Alé La Merckx! 

Un nuovo percorso che parte dal centro di Livigno e arrivo a Livigno Mottolino, sulle ormai del Giro d’Italia, del Cannibale e più di recente di Tadej Pogačar. 

Due percorsi, Granfondo (166,8 km and 4.300 m dislivello) e Mediofondo (106,8 km e 2.050 m dislivello), per attraversare scenari fantastici e un territorio che ha legato a doppio filo la sua storia con la storia del ciclismo.

Un nuovo capitolo di questo storico evento sta per iniziare: scopriamo insieme tutte le novità!

Iscriviti ora su https://www.endu.net/it/events/granfondo-livigno/

In collaborazione con Livigno

~
00:00 Nuova partenza
00:56 Il Centro Olimpico di Acquagranda
02:27 Un percorso con Passi mitici
03:55 La magia di Livigno
05:14 Subito in salita
06:46 Il Passo Bernina
08:14 Scalata tra le gallerie
09:56 Ultime, durissime rampe
11:18 Una cartolina magnifica
12:21 Segna in agenda: 29 giugno

~

Trovi consigli di esperti, racconti di imprese, aneddoti divertenti e tanti stimoli per migliorare come ciclista, runner, nuotatore, triatleta e amante degli sport di montagna. 
Mettiti alla prova, raggiungi con noi i tuoi prossimi obiettivi!

eligibility.notes: 2026 edition ran 27 June 2026 as planned with 850+ starters confirmed by official post-event report; ENDU registration now closed. Event dropped the 'La Merckx' branding used in its 2024 debut edition.
